comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes past, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I'll have a post on this on the blog in due time.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my thirty-first published book; I've been covering some of my less successful titles of late, but this isn't one of those -- not by a long shot. Years before, I'd begun to stumble across scraps of information about groups in Britain between the wars that combined the symbolism and teachings of the Druid Revival with the magical techniques of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. Those went out of fashion after the Second World War with the rise of Wicca, and very little apparently survived of their teachings and traditions. When it finally became clear to me that I wasn't going to be able to get any of the teachings of these groups, I set out to reverse engineer a comparable system, using my own background in Druidry and the Golden Dawn as a basis. This was the first of several books that resulted. It's a complete curriculum of ceremonial magic using Golden Dawn methods and Druid Revival symbolism, and it's attracted an active community of practitioners, which you can find (should you be interested) here. The book? It's still very much in print and readily available; you can get a copy here.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Review of Prayer: a History and Question on Fiction Relevant to Occult Philosophy
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A happy early Summer Sunstead (Solstice) to all who mark it!

To Share:

1) I posted a book review that might be of more general interest to folks here on Prayer: a History by Philip and Carol Zaleski. Overall, I had mixed feelings, but I think it will prove a good jumping off point for further research: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_prayer_a_history

2) Possibly related to my asking below, but I've just started a short novel called The Turquoise Serpent by Alexander Palacio (twitter handle: conan_esq). It's apparently meso-American-flavored sword and sorcery, and as the author's handle might suggest, the influence of Robert E. Howard is strong, but in an interview, he also mentioned Jack Vance, Gene Wolfe, and some others as shaping his writing. I'm only about 20 pages in, but already I think folks here might enjoy it.

To Ask: Other than your own, of course, what fiction do you think is the most interesting/inspiring/useful from an occult philosophy standpoint? I can guess a few titles you might mention that have come up repeatedly, but I'm curious what else might come to mind. I also, of course, welcome any recommendations from the commentariat.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of Prayer: a History and Question on Fiction Relevant to Occult Philosophy
replies.content: In terms of fiction that has a meaningful occult-philosophy dimension, there isn't much. Dion Fortune's later novels -- The Goat Foot God, The Winged Bull, The Sea Priestess, and Moon Magic -- are worth reading from that perspective. CS Lewis's adult fiction and Charles Williams' novels include bits of genuine if half-understood occult philosophy in a thick paste of Anglican Christian theology; Aleister Crowley's novels have the same thing, but in his case the thick paste is his own rebel-without-a-clue autolatrous theology. Here and there you can find scraps of old science fiction and fantasy with good stuff in them, but it's a bit here and a bit there in whole books. In terms of older literature, those of the original Grail legends that have Perceval/Parzival as their hero are good stuff -- if Galahad's the hero, it's been rewritten by monks to erase the esoteric content and replace it with Catholic theology. The Hypnerotomachia Poliphilii is also good, and available in an English translation. But that's what comes to mind at the moment.

replies.title: Re: Review of Prayer: a History and Question on Fiction Relevant to Occult Philosophy
replies.content: Which one of the Fortune novels do you think is the best one? I've read about half of The Sea Priestess, and I must confess I find her to be a pretty bad novelist.

To my mind, The Goat Foot God is her best -- though here again it's not a page-turner. Read them for the occult philosophy, not the literary quality.

replies.content:
It's Meyrink.
His books are pretty amazing. The fellow who does the Hermetix podcast just reviewed Meyrink's best known book "The Golem" recently. All of his occult themed novels have now been given good translations by Mike Mitchell.
Meyrink was a member of a number of occult organizations, including the Theosophical society, and, by reputation, at least one old German Rosenkreutzer order. I suspect that he kept a careful dream journal and used his dreams to build his fiction which has a somewhat unsettling quality, but he also was as impressed with the society he lived in as I am of the one I live in, so he had a very developed sense of sarcasm which makes some of his stuff hilarious (how about meeting the Wandering Jew in a magic shop that sell pornographic novels disguised as dull Victorian fare. That's in "The Green Face").
They're great books, but a little challenging. "The Golem" had an earlier translation that Dover has republished recently, but the newer translations are published by Dedalus, and the Folio Society has done a typically beautiful edition of Mitchell's translation of the Golem.

replies.content:
They're not fiction in the strict sense, but Lafcadio Hearn's collections of Japanese folktales contain a tremendous amount of occult lore if you look carefully, and I recommend them. Some good examples are the importance of one's final thoughts, the principle of substitution, ghost-lore and why necromancy is a bad idea, and why you can't use spirituality to run from your problems. As someone for whom life is very difficult, I found this story of karma inspiring.

If there's any difficulty, here, it's that these are disconnected anecdotes; you will need to have a ready framework to slot them into in order to make sense of them.

replies.title: Re: Review of Prayer: a History and Question on Fiction Relevant to Occult Philosophy
replies.content:
If I may... I love Lafcadio Hearn as well, and wrote this ode to him a number of years ago:

DREAMER OF THE DARK FANTASTIC
AN ODE FOR KOIZUMI YAKUMO: PATRICK LAFCADIO HEARN

Lafcadio,
your Irish eyebrows
pour over paper
while your classic Greek hand
unfurls spindly writing
beneath diminutive lamplight.

Your one good eye is scrunched, myopic
your fingers cramped, back sore
head full of fantasy and folk lore,
the perfumed dreams of a fairy in a Tea garden.

You were a spiritual ambassador
for your final home in Japan,
adopted by the Kami as much by the people
from your birthplace on Lefkada,
by way of Dublin, Cincinnati
the West Indies and New Orleans.

Your gift was to tease out
the soul of a place,
to translate the genius loci
into a liquid language
soluble to friends
left behind in the West.

You were locked away as child,
shut in a closet, a punishment
to cure fears of the dark.
With age you end up going nearly blind,
befriending many ghosts along the way.

Your gift was inner sight.
In a playground game you lost an eye
but like Odin, became a seer
peering into Other worlds,
more at home in the Mittelmarch
then under the smoky towers of industry.
Your pen was made of foxfire
and all your words were goblin.

Washed up penniless in the Queen City
you slept on paper shavings
at the local printers,
nourishing your dreams in the library,
breathing in a host of fantastic heterodoxies.

Bootstrapped up from the pavement
you eventually landed behind a news desk.
Never afraid
you stared down the barrel of horror,
from violent cremations to tan yard murders;
immersing yourself in haunted landscapes,
listening for echoes, voices of ancient ancestors.

You did not mind bucking authority
even when it bucked you back.
Three glasses down, coming home from the beer garden
into the kitchen at the boarding house,
your eyes fell on Mattie, a black skinned storyteller.
The world wasn’t ready for your illegal love,
cast upon the rocks of social disdain
you couldn’t reach a safe harbor,
and your marriage, void under law, dissolved.

So you slinked on down to New Orleans
and simmered yourself in Creole cuisine,
hacking away at the papers again,
scribbling down many a fanciful sketch,
making attempts at translations from French,
growing your powers, pouring your light
into newsprint, denouncing corruption,
disabusing fallacy, penning obituaries
for Doctor John and Marie Laveau.

Then tiring of the Southern scene,
waxing weary of the socials while wanting the weird
you traveled even deeper south,
skipping off for two years to Martinique,
where you wooed the Muse of the odd
in search of tropical flavors
to quill your cryptic travelogues.

Then off again, pit stopping
in New York to wrangle with editors, publishers,
magazine men;
before tacking along on a train to Vancouver
to step aboard the good ship Abyssinia
on a Pacific passage dark as any of yours
and step off on St. Patrick’s day in Japan 1890,
Yokohoma, among people who smiled,
wishing you well during the days torrential rain
dreaming at night of ideographs and insects
soundless visions running on phantom wheels.

In Matsue
you married during the frozen winter
Setsu the warm daughter of a Samurai,
who melted the coarser aspects of your nature.
There wasn’t much language between you
so she spoke in the shared sympathy of Shinto
in the grave voice of her countries oldest chillers,
breathing life into ghosts,
awakening cruel demons who slept close to Earth.

Finding home at last you were made a citizen,
became professor Yakumo
gave birth to a son Kazuo
unleashed yourself on paper
letting the local spirits do the writing,
as your blood boiled under oil lamplight,
possessed, prolific, a passionate interpreter.

On good terms with Kitsune
the last years of your life were happy.

You made a boat of words
to crest over this rocky world of men,
and resting, sailed beyond them

your final voyage
off to visit the Hare in the moon
one last stop
on the river to Heaven.


--Justin Patrick Moore

comments.title: Mushroom metaphysics
comments.content: Hi JMG, I recall someone once asking you about using mushrooms as magical and/or meditative foci, and you advised caution because they are symbols of decay and may bring about such influences when invoked. Many mushrooms do feed on decaying material but others form symbiotic relationships with plants (see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mycorrhiza). The research cited at the link suggests that fungi may have been instrumental in helping plants first establish themselves on land and that mycorrhizal fungi not only provide nutrients to plants but also help to augment their immune systems. In light of this, do you see mushrooms as having a universal association with decay? Is this an aspect of their spiritual character regardless of their varied ecological niches? The mycologist Paul Stamets has an interesting theory that organisms with an affinity for fungi tend to survive evolutionary bottlenecks and they have many benefits to human health so it's hard for me to see them as having such an entropic resonance.

replies.title: Re: Mushroom metaphysics
replies.content: The traditional lore in Western occultism on that subject is what it is. If you don't want to listen to it, hey, do what keepeth thou from wilting shall be the loophole in the law, and all that, but that doesn't change the fact that the traditional lore -- crafted by people who lived much closer to nature than you and I -- says what it says.

comments.title: Elements in SoP
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I read your introduction with the note about evocation of spirits, and thank you, I agree; but this question is directly concerning the Sphere of Protection. I hope you'll indulge me...

I have been wrestling with the subject of evocation of spirits, which also offends me, in regards to the SoP. I decided that I would avoid the phrase "invocation of elements", and use the term "evoke" instead; as the former seemed to imply that whole approach of conjuring the elements and forcing spirits to obey my will. "Evoke" relates to the usage: "evoke a memory", implying that the spirit is already with you. It can manifest at its own will, if it wants. And I am the element's co-worker -- even friend in the process of the SoP. I have decided that adopting a sitting posture with the SoP makes me feel less aggressive.

But my question has to do with the cosmic structure of the elements in the SoP. After some thought, I came up with the idea that the elements of course have a personal, microcosmic form within my own body. I access these forms first.

The elements of my body correspond, I believe, to a higher, macrocosmic type of being or form which may or may not transcend the spirits of the elements. I access these forms in the four directions.

Finally, I came up with a notion of a connection and correspondence with the Divine, at a level I call "Principles". The Principles that exist here I conceive of being within the Mind of Spirit, but the Principles contacted here would relate in the highest way with the earthly elements... Here I feel I can somehow connect with the Divine itself, too.

Thoughts on this? any connections with existing occult theory? Other than perhaps Swedenborg or maybe Plato? I haven't read much hermeticism.

Thanks for this space for us, Mr. Greer; and thanks for your valuable help!

Take Care.

First, I recommend working with the existing phrasing for a year before you start tinkering with it, so you know what you're doing. I'm also a little puzzled by the fact that you say you find evocation unwelcome but you want to use the word "evoke" in the ritual! The point of "invoke" is that it literally means "call in" (in-vocare) -- you are calling the elemental force into yourself. To evoke is to "call out" (ex-vocare), which is not what you want to do here.

Second, there are many ways to talk about the levels of being that transcend the material. I recommend doing the work and experiencing them before doing too much theorizing about them.

replies.title: Invocation and Evocation
replies.content:
Along these lines I just came across a paragraph in a book on this subject. The book is A Path to the Grail, a book that shares Dion Fortune's papers about The Church of the Graal with complementary essays by Christian Gilson.

Christian writes in reference to the ritual of the Guild of the Master Jesus Dion describes in her papers:
"It is important here for the initiate to remember that this is an act of invocation and not evocation. We should not be expecting a concentrated manifestation, if such a thing is possible. Rather we are using the mind to create a form into which the Divine force can flow. The nature of that force will depend entirely on the focus of our intention. The understanding that we bring to the form will effectively tune the form to receive the appropriate force. This is why the initiate needs training in the effective use of symbolism. For force cannot be held in the wrong form, or an incomplete one, it will flow out, or destroy the form, just as water entering a broken chalice will force the cracks open and through its pressure shatter the chalice."

I practice the SOP in the FHR form daily and Christian's writing here more generally helps me understand the importance of sticking with the SOP as written and trusting that with repetition alongside meditation, I am changing my personal form in order to be more receptive to the energies I am invoking (forces) through the SOP. It really is a process of trust and consistency for me which was initially very challenging and is getting easier as I build a deeper relationship with myself and the energies I am invoking.

comments.title: Analogies and cycles
comments.content:
1) A few thoughts to share about physical analogies - maybe you find something to comment on or somebody else finds interest in reading it: The Kybalion presents an analogy, describing a physical object spinning with increasing velocity. It's claimed that at the beginning, we hear a sound of increasing frequency, then come effects like heat radiation of increasing intensity (though it would be more appropriate to talk about increasing temperature), then comes (visible) light, and so on. The point of the analogy is to make plausible that the planes are a continuous spectrum, as the Kybalion claims. But as far as my understanding goes, if you take a closer look at the physical effects at work the analogy demonstrates that the planes are NOT continuous: a) Sound is created by the interaction of dense matter (the object) with slightly more subtle matter (air). Heat is created by the interaction by these to kinds of matter, but it effects the subtle part of the object, namely the atoms and molecules of which it is composed. These subtle parts interact with a much more subtle matter, the ether, vacuum, or whatever you like to call it. This is completely distinct to creation of sound. Heat radiation can of course turn visible, but it's still the same effect. Then there's probably the direct interaction of the subtle parts of matter (atoms, molecules) with the ether by reason of them being part of the object which rotates at very high speed which could cause synchrotron radation or and other effects - again, this works on an entirely different "plane". And so on.

What's interesting is that the different physical sub-planes seem to come into being by interaction of more subtle parts of matter forming gross parts. And then new effects (like sound, light) are created by interaction between the subtle and the gross which is very reminiscent of the CosDoc's "movement of movement".

2) An actual question: I've noticed that in many projects one faces a phase of creation, then comes a point where a part of what you have created "evaporates" with a "solid nucleus" remaining. Sometimes this can be voluntarily, for example when you have constructed something and clean up afterwards. But sometimes, especially with projects of a higher grade of complexity, it can happen that you lose a part of what you have created and regress to an earlier stage but on a higher level. An example: We keep chicken. At the beginning, we had a rather small enclosure for a short time, but for various reasons we changed our way of keeping chicken in various ways. Now, as a result of some distinct setbacks, we are back to the enclosure. But this time, the enclosure - as a result of our experience - is much better than the first instance. However, we had to give up many of our ideas and plans on how to keep chicken, since they have proven to be not practicable. This, too, is very reminiscent of the CosDoc describing cosmic phases.

Now I guess that many of this pases could be correlated to astrological constellations. If this is true, is it possible to sketch in very broad terms, what you need to look out for? In the case of my example, I could imagine Saturn playing role since chicken keeping is an agricultural activity?

Thank you, as always!
Cheers,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: Intelligent EBE's, Contact
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Do you believe there are conscious intelligent beings that inhabit other planets in our solar system, if not on the physical plane then on the etheric and higher planes? On other solar systems and galaxies throughout the cosmos? Might some live on the physical plane but far beneath the surfaces of the planets? Have there been any authentic communications between them and humanity? If they exist, are these civilizations much more evolved than present humanity? Is there a "ring-pass-not" in operation that disallows/forbids any contact or communication by us with ET's/EBE's existing beyond our solar system? Are you familiar with Omnec Onec who wrote The Venusian Trilogy and claims Venusian origin, and do you give her story any credence? Do the accounts of Valiant Thor from Venus in the 1950's have any validity?

Thank you kindly in advance for your response!

replies.title: Re: Intelligent EBE's, Contact
replies.content:
In occult teaching the universe is full of intelligent beings. Only a fairly small fraction of them have bodies made of our kind of matter, and our planet is said to be the only one in this solar system that has intelligent beings in material bodies. There are far more solar systems out there than our astronomers know about; according to Dion Fortune's Cosmic Doctrine, the plane that we can access with our telescopes, the seventh Cosmic plane, is only one of seven such planes, each of which is as full of star systems as our plane -- it's quite possible that the others account for the "dark matter" that has physicists so baffled. (It's not actually dark, we just can't see it.)

Each solar system, however, is an isolated unit pursuing its own process of spiritual evolution under the guidance of its own Solar Logos (or Logoi, in a multiple-star system). Direct contact between solar systems is avoided because it could lead to serious imbalances; even indirect contacts cause significant disruptions. Since there are beings pursuing their own spiritual evolution on all the planets, though, each solar system is crowded.

I haven't read up on Omnec Onec. The Valiant Thor story's a fascinating one, though I'm by no means sure it's literally true -- many of these accounts need to be read as spiritual parables rather than literal descriptions of fact. I've come to think that a lot of the early contactees had spiritual rather than physical interactions with intelligent, non-biological entities from elsewhere in our solar system, but presented them as physical contacts so that people would take their teachings seriously.

Still, the cohesiveness and longevity of the storms provide some structure. Sometimes, over the course of time, one storm will overtake and engulf another one. Is this a battle? Is it communication? Is it some sort of genetic exchange that serves the same function as our procreation does? It all seems very intriguing to me!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Intelligent EBE's, Contact

As for what benefits might be gained from these contacts, when you say "we could put into practice," it all depends on which "we" you have in mind. 2.5) Trees vary in how they relate to human beings; some species have energies that aren't healthy for us. replies.title: Re: Trees, part II

It's not just the parrticular cypress rees you lived by. Gerald Durrell, the writer, spent part of his childhood on the Grek island Corfu, and wrote My Family and Other Animals (1956) about his experience there. Here's a quote from it about how one islander warned him against ever sleeping under a cypress:

'Ah, you may sit under them, yes. They cast a good shadow, cold as well-water; but that's the trouble, they tempt you to sleep. And you must never, for any reason, sleep beneath a cypress.' He paused, stroked his moustache, waited for me to ask why, and then went on: 'Why? Why? Because if you did you would be changed when you woke. Yes, the black cypresses, they are dangerous. While you sleep, their roots grow into your brains and steal them, and when you wake up you are mad, head as empty as a whistle.' I asked whether it was only the cypress that could do that or did it apply to other trees. 'No, only the cypress,' said the old man, peering up fiercely at the trees above me as though to see whether they were listening; 'only the cypress is the thief of intelligence. So be warned, little lord, and don't sleep here.'

replies.title: Re: Trees, part II
replies.content:
Regarding the healthfulness of pines and firs, it’s interesting that the name ‘fir’ in English, and the words for ‘pine’ (as well as ‘oak’) in various Indo-European languages all go back to a Proto-Indo-European word ‘perkwus’ meaning ‘life-soul.’ That word in turn is linked to the name of the Proto-Indo-European Thunder God, Perkwunos. For example, pine is Fohre (with an umlaut, sounds like fir) in modern German. Latin quercus, the oak, has the same root. Linguistically, our life-souls are linked with specific trees.

I discuss this further in my article “Born of Trees and Thunder: The Ferah Soul” on my website HeathenSoulLore.net. Note: due to sound-shifts during language evolution, the sound ‘p’ shifts to ‘f’ in some languages, and ‘k’ shifts to ‘h’, hence the shift from ‘perkwus’ to Gothic ‘fairhw,’ Anglo-Saxon feorh, etc., descending to modern fir and Fohre.

Winifred Hodge Rose

I substantially agree with John.

That said, the Buddha taught the Jhanas, or at least the lower ones, as something relatively easy for meditators to enter, and a more pleasant subject of absorption (state) than, say, sitting still and just watching the breath for thousands of hours by willpower alone. (Just try sitting, without moving, for five -- or twenty-five -- hours without them)

Supposedly, the Buddha had mastered the Jhanas during his years of studying advanced yoga under various teachers. He ultimately found them to not provide the path to Nirvana, by themselves, some promoted them as providing. They remained, though, as something he strongly advocated his students cultivate as a tool for more advanced meditation.

What is interesting is how the Jhanas then disappeared, almost entirely, from the toolbox of techniques taught to most practicing Buddhists. Priesthoods are like that -- they want to be "special" and necessary -- and they can't have people exploring spirituality without them!

I browsed through the post quickly. It's kind of interesting, and it may be possible. But it is kind of hard to say what the author of the article actually experienced. If you consider that the Jhanas are actually states of hypnotic trance, that explains John's reservations. What makes me further suspicious is (a) although a few decades ago it was hard to find decent information on the Jhanas, increasingly there is information, rehashed, everywhere (b) the author says she quickly got bored of experiencing what should be EXTREME states of bliss, which some meditators find highly addictive and (c) the context of the other geek articles in the blog make it hard for me to take the author terribly seriously as a quasi-enlightened being.

N./9-12: Fort.Min, Cauda Drac., Laetitia, Puer

replies.title: Re: Geomancy interpretation
replies.content: This is a great example of why overthinking a chart is the major barrier to interpretation. Your judge and witnesses are wholly positive, and there's a mode of perfection -- translation -- connecting the house of the quesited to that of the querent. The other things in the chart do not matter. The answer is "yes."

replies.title: Re: Geomancy interpretation
replies.content:
Thank you!
Overthinking is a vice of mine.
Ironically, I first picked up divination as a way to escape "paralysis by analysis". I'm glad I can provide an object lesson, at least.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy interpretation
replies.content: It's a very common problem with geomancy, which is very simple and straightforward. The rule to follow with yes or no questions, if you're using a shield chart, is to take the judge and witnesses for your answer; if you're using a house chart, take the presence or absence of a mode of perfection connecting the figures of querent and quesited for your answer. Everything else is a minor factor. The one thing I'd suggest in terms of the broader chart is that you may want to explore the possibility that stress related to money (2nd house) and marriage (7th house), and possibly to a former career (10th house), may have helped cause your health problem; appropriate adjustments with regard to those may help.

replies.title: Re: Transits for daily divination

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
John Opsopaus discusses the Greek and Hellenistic approach to this in some of his books (the Oracles of Apollo and the Oracles of Homer and the Bones); very briefly, he says that they divided divination methods into two classes, natural divination (dreams, visions, omens, etc. all under the rulership of Apollo) and artificial divination (dice, cards, etc. all under the rulership of Hermes). The former was considered to be inspired and comes from above when it will; the latter was considered to be a skill and practiced as such.

Assuming their distinction was accurate, it seems unlikely that one can try to force such inspiration on a regular basis, since it is not a skill and can't be practiced (though I suppose one can work on growing more receptive).

comments.title: The Seal of Alchemy
comments.content:
Hello!
I was intrigued by a geometric drawing called the seal of alchemy. It consists of a circle within a square within an equilateral triangle within a larger circle. Its quite a fascinating and elegant drawing. It took me quite some time and meditation to figure out how to draw it with a ruler and compass only. Now I am trying to figure out its meaning now. Internet searches reveal the following:

(1) It’s called the philosopher’s seal or alchemical emblem.

Large Circle: Represents the infinite and eternal nature of the Universe.

Triangle: Represents the 3 alchemical elements of sulfur (soul), mercury(spirit), salt(body). *Think the Trinity in Christianity or Mind, Body, Soul.

Square: 4 Elements, each point touches the inside of the triangle representing Earth, Air, Fire, Water

Small Circle: Represents the 5th Element, Spirit/Aether or the “quintessence” the element believed to be the substance that permeates all things in the Universe and connects everything together. In some interpretations it’s also symbolic of the Sun, associated with the alchemical element of gold, representing attainment of higher consciousness.

The combination of everything inside the large circle symbolizes the unity and harmony of the Universe. The seal itself is often used for meditation and contemplation, and is about personal growth and spiritual transformation, the goal being to achieve spiritual enlightenment and unlock the secrets of the Universe. "
https://www.reddit.com/r/Hermeticism/comments/11uvouc/what_does_this_symbol_meanrepresent/?rdt=63886

or
(2) Alchemy's ultimate purpose was to create the stone. Many alchemists, including notable scientists like Isaac Newton, worked hard to figure out the recipe.

The philosopher's stone symbolizes enlightenment, heavenly happiness, and perfection in alchemy.

The symbol is a circle inside a square. Around them is a triangle inside another circle.

https://hellofearless.com/blogs/blog/23-most-common-alchemy-symbol

What would be your take on the inner meanings of that diagram?
And yes, I am continuing my meditations on that matter.
Thanks and regards

replies.title: Re: The Seal of Alchemy
replies.content:
Oh, that. Here's the original:
[IMAGE:0]
It's emblem 21 (of 50) of Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens, which you can find online in its complete form here:

https://furnaceandfugue.org/atalanta-fugiens/emblem21.html

It includes the image, a musical fugue, a poem, and a text. Some modern alchemists pulled the image out of context, deleted the two figures in the center, changed the geometry, ignored all the material that Maier himself wrote about it, and started slapping their own meanings onto it. I'm delighted that you took the time to learn how to construct it geometrically -- that's a worthwhile task -- but the image as it's been redesigned isn't part of traditional alchemy at all.

replies.title: increase in transgender
replies.content:
There are IMO several factors at work. Changes in definition--people who used to be called transvestites, who got some sort of sexual satisfaction from wearing the clothes of the opposite sex are now encouraged to think of themselves as transgender. Some label them as autogynophiliacs. That is, they receive satisfaction from imagining themselves as female, dressing as female and being recognized as female, but don't necessarily wish to have sexual reassignment surgery. Or as a lesbian friend of mine puts it, "Don't want to give up the magic wand." I don't think the numbers of actual early onset gender dysphoria (the traditional transsexual) has increased by much. And in the nations that are permitting self-identification for prisoners the motive for a man to wish to be placed in a woman's prison is pretty obvious, especially for sex criminals.

Follow the money--physicians used to be reluctant to give hormones and especially to perform sexual reassignment surgery. And insurance didn't cover it. That has changed to some degree from portions of the Affordable Care act. Hormones create a lifelong patient for the pharmaceutical companies. I just read an article about The March of Dimes Effect--when a problem is solved, as polio was with the invention of vaccines, the activists need another cause--in the case of the March of Dimes that was the also laudable goal of decreasing birth defects. But in the case of the LGB etc. movement the first goal was to end anti-sodomy laws. When that was accomplished, the goal was reset for marriage equality. Goal accomplished, so now it is freedom, and equal whatever for every sexual minority or identity that can be imagined.

Third, the increase in young girls identifying as trans is partly social contagion--the stresses of puberty are pretty bad for girls in our society. Conflicting advice and goals on every side. Easy to see that being a boy might seem easier. Not that it is, but the adults pushing this stuff are not citing the high male suicide rate, addiction, depression, etc. There is growing evidence that many of the girls "diagnosed" as trans have a constellation of other, unaddressed and undiagnosed problems ranging from autism, to sexual abuse, ACHD.

None of this rules out occult explanations of course and the internet makes information and pressure available that was unthinkable in my youth or the youth of my children. I am relieved that the tide of opinion on this seems to be turning, but still am anxious about my granddaughter.

comments.content:
I wanted to say thank you to everyone who helped me out on last Magic Monday. I suppose some of my messages of gratitude did not go through (and I was unwilling to re-write them in a PM as they were rather emotional to get through the writing itself) since they were past the time closure for comments. However, I have read and re-read everything that was written and used them as emotional support for this very raw time I find myself in.

To re-iterate: I have been doing the SOP for about a year and reading this blog which has helped me process most of what occurred to me as an unvaccinated RN on the front lines in California during the lockdowns. Pretty traumatic time for me but I found that there was some inner strength that guided me through the ordeal and possibly to this website to eventually learn about occult teachings which now seem more prescient than ever before. Occult teachings were the only thing that made sense in those times, above and beyond all philosophical moors I had up until that point. I have recently begun discursive meditation and it seems to be affecting me in a way that it never had prior. At first it was exciting in a way, and I was elated to see measurable differences in the equanimity of my mood, my ability to concentrate, and deliberate a thought all the way through.

This past week and a half though, I have been haunted by visions of what our host so poetically calls "meat-puppets being jerked around in space" or alternatively "The Watcher at the Gate".

This experience has been so painful I took our host's advice and continued my meditations otherwise I was warned I would be stuck in this state of this frighteningly haunting consciousness. In this period of time I have come to the interesting conclusion that I think this state of nihilism is perhaps encouraged in many modern interpretations of Buddhism as - in my struggle to understand what I am experiencing - I found a lot of modern Buddhist voices reinforcing my experience that this feeling of emptiness is the ground of reality and enlightened state of being. This is terribly frightening for me: on one hand I can see that there truly is "emptiness' in the sense that matter is not as solid as a materialistic world vision would have me believe. On the other hand...uff...it is hard to explain what the experience of looking out into such a world truly is. But I know that I DO NOT LIKE IT.

So I continue to meditate. Because I can see that I am battling my rational mind in some ways I have had to turn to a philosopher that appeals to my rational mind. The author of Materialism is Baloney - Bernardo Katsrup - has been extremely helpful for me to see the balance between this emptiness/consciousness. Furthermore, he has had lead me to re-read Schopenhauer with a more critical eye. It seems to me that Schopenhauer out of all the modern philosophers truly helps to refute the meat puppet experience with his idea of will. I see the concept of will also reflected in occult studies.

So on to my query after this long-winded preface: What is the occult view of the Will? Bernardo talks about how he walked through ego-death with equanimity once he realized that there was something witnessing/experiencing the sense of "I am dying" (his ego-death brought about by a psychedelic trip which I most certainly do not have the wherewithal to do). He also stated that he walked through re-entering ego with the thought of "I am being born". Both states he could feel SOMETHING witnessing these birth/death processes even when he did not have the formal/cognitive capacity of ego. Is this thing that is witnessing even when the ego is gone...is that the Will? Is that the spirit/individuality that is re-incarnated or is that still the lower form of our spirit/personality that we will lose upon death?

These questions seem vitally important to me at this stage because they point to a livingness in everything. I still don't feel as if I experience the world in this way at all times; but if I have a will in the way that Schopenhauer/Katsrup describes: it seems to be the same thing that is looking out from everything/one else.

Thank you again for all of your time. This has been an emotional/cognitive ride for me in ways that I can hardly explain but that I feel compelled to work through. I have heard our host before say that IT finds you. That seems to be the case for me here.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Schopenhauer was a major influence on Eliphas Lévi -- from the world as will and representation to magic as will and imagination is a very short step! -- and through him to the magical tradition in general. That is to say, you're following a very traditional route! The great difference between Schopenhauer's ideas and those of classic Western occultism is that in the latter, the Will is understood to differentiate into semi-separate individualities -- think of the way your fingers branch off from your hand as a metaphor for this.

Each of the countless "fingers" of the Will is an individuality, the self you had before your mother and father were born; that "finger" then puts on one "glove" called the personality, a second "glove" over it called the ego, and a third "glove" over that called the body. Thus you can shed your body, as of course you do when you die, and you can shed your ego, and you can shed your personality; you can take off one or two or three gloves; but the finger's still there, a differentiated current of the primal Will.

Consciousness is how the Will reacts when it encounters an obstacle. What obstacles can it encounter? Only other differentiated expressions of itself, since all that is, is Will. So where there is consciousness, there is differentiated will, individuality, that which abides.

comments.title: The Sub-Lunar Realm
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG and all!

Would you share your understanding of the nature of the sub-lunar realm as seen in medieval and later occultism? Is this identical to the Astral plane, or related but different? Are the beings who populate it astral-level beings, perhaps astral-elementals? Or are they beings who exist on additional levels besides the astral, and are more complex than elementals? Maybe both kinds exist there?

Thank you for your thoughts, and for hosting this forum!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The Sub-Lunar Realm
replies.content: The sublunar realm in medieval and Renaissance tradition is the realm of the four elements. It's equivalent to the material and etheric planes; the astral realm, as the name suggests, is the world of the stars and planets, from the Moon up to the sphere of fixed stars. The beings who inhabit the sublunar realm are humans, animals, demons, and nature spirits (fairies, fauns, etc.). If you haven't done so already, I highly recommend reading CS Lewis's The Discarded Image, which covers all this stuff, and then the Cosmographia of Bernardus Sylvestris, Lewis's main source for his planetary trilogy, which is readily available in English translation.

replies.title: Re: The Sub-Lunar Realm
replies.content:
Thank you! Would the elementals be included among the nature spirits there, or are they a separate Order? And I think they envisioned lower-order angels operating through the sub-lunar realm, didn’t they, who carried messages back & forth from humans and greater beings outside the Moon’s radius? How about the souls of the human dead?

Well, thanks for the references; I’ll follow up. I’ve greatly enjoyed rereading Lewis’s Space Trilogy over decades since my teens, and will be interested to get more background from the reading you recommend.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The Sub-Lunar Realm
replies.content:
Medieval and early Renaissance sources don't include the concept of elementals. That's an idea that emerged in the very late Renaissance, with Paracelsus as the most important figure. You'd have to read his work to see.

Angels of all of the six lower choirs pass through the sublunary realm but don't belong to it. As for the souls of the human dead, the virtuous go to the supercelestial realm, and the vicious go to the subterrestrial realm -- yes, that's spelled "Hell" in plain English. It's below the elemental realms, as heaven is above it.

comments.title: natually mummified euro body tattoos may be accupucture 2000 yrs before known use in china
comments.content:
JMG

Not a question, but a tidbit of info that probably won’t surprise you, and you may already know, but you and other’s interested in various traditional chinese medicine may find of interest.

Namely, a ~5000 year old naturally mummified body found in Austria/Italy Alps has tattoos on body locations where there’s indication of pain, and that also correspond to locations of meridians and other concepts from accupucture/accupressure, and ~2000 years before the earliest known use in China.

To my mind, this would seem to indicate a separate derivation of the knowledge, and not unscientific “woo” as some would claim. (I suppose it’s also possible the knowledge was passed around the world.) Either way, seems to throw a monkey wrench in few “rationalist” claims, so I thought it may be of interest to you and your readers.

A couple excerpted quotes from the wikipedia article about him. (Also, some interesting info not relevant to the above, about how analyzing his stomach for food and pollen etc, they can derive an amazing amount of info about how he spent his last 2 days of life. )

-----
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%96tzi

Ötzi, also called the Iceman, is the natural mummy of a man who lived between 3350 and 3105 BC. Ötzi's remains were discovered on 19 September 1991, in the Ötztal Alps (hence the nickname "Ötzi", German: [œtsi]) at the Austria–Italy border. He is Europe's oldest known natural human mummy, offering an unprecedented view of Chalcolithic (Copper Age) Europeans.

-----


-----
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%96tzi#Skeletal_details_and_tattooing

Ötzi had a total of 61 tattoos, consisting of 19 groups of black lines [… details not relevant to the summary excluded …]

Radiological examination of Ötzi's bones showed "age-conditioned or strain-induced degeneration" corresponding to many tattooed areas, including osteochondrosis and slight spondylosis in the lumbar spine and wear-and-tear degeneration in the knee and especially in the ankle joints.[35] It has been speculated that these tattoos may have been part of pain relief treatments similar to acupressure or acupuncture,[32] though Ötzi lived at least 2,000 years before their previously known earliest use in China ( 1000 BC).[36] For example, 9 of the 19 groups of his tattoos are located next to, or directly on, acupunctural areas used today, and most of the others are on meridians and other acupunctural regions of the body and over arthritic joints. Ötzi's abdominal tattoos may have assuaged the intestinal pain of whipworm, which he is thought to have had.[34][37]
At one point, it was thought that Ötzi was the oldest tattooed human mummy yet discovered.[38][39] In 2018, however, tattoos were discovered on nearly contemporaneous Egyptian mummies.[40]

-----
